editableNewDialogue.text: I’m switching topics again—can you help me think through a quick decision?
Sure. What decision are you weighing, and what are the options?
Say I have two ways to present the same information: a short bullet list or a tiny story. Which one usually keeps attention better?

In general: bullets win for clarity and fast scanning; a tiny story wins for emotional pull and recall. If attention is the main goal, open with a micro-story (1–2 sentences), then switch to bullets to deliver the info efficiently.

Usually 20–60 seconds when spoken, or ~2–5 short sentences in text. It should contain: a specific moment, a small tension/problem, and a concrete outcome. Example: “The train doors were closing, and I realized I’d dropped the ticket. I panicked—then noticed it stuck to my sleeve. I laughed, got on, and decided to always use a wallet clip.” Then you list the takeaways.
Give me a template I can reuse.

Template:
1) Setting (where/when, 1 line)
2) Trigger (what changed, 1 line)
3) Stakes (why it mattered, 1 line)
4) Action (what you did, 1 line)
5) Result (what happened, 1 line)
6) Lesson (bridge into bullets, 1 line)

Optional: time-box it with a rule like “no more than 120 words.”

(Aside: In a recent museum exhibit redesign case study, labels were shortened to ~60 words and paired with one anecdote per room, and visitor dwell time increased—useful as a general pattern, regardless of topic.)

Okay then—today after class I had to head straight to the hotel again, so I’m thinking of filming a "what fits in my backpack" kind of thing, but it’s basically just gloves, a couple of microfiber cloths, and my uniform.

That’s a strong everyday hook because it’s specific to your routine and still stays anonymous. You could frame it as a quick “minimal kit” video: what you carry, why each item matters, and a few silent B-roll shots of prep (no logos/guest areas).
By the way, small correction from earlier: I’m not actually living in Kyoto anymore. I moved to Osaka last month, but I still commute to Kyoto for language school on weekdays.
Got it—so your current home base is Osaka, and Kyoto is where you go for school during the week. That changes the framing from “Kyoto daily life” to “commuter life between Osaka and Kyoto.”

annotation.queryText: I’m filling out a delivery form for a local service and it asks which city I should select as my current residence so they can calculate availability and fees—what should I pick based on where I’m actually living?

subAnnotations.queryText: I'm thinking of making a video series about Kyoto's hidden spots that even locals might not know. Since I walk to school every day and work at a hotel, I see a lot of places tourists miss. What kind of editing style would work best for showing these places while keeping my identity private?
